entries.description: OmX - Oh My codeX: Your codex is not alone. Add hooks, agent teams, HUDs, and so much more.
entries.takeaway: Hook system for AI coding agents with composable agent teams and a terminal HUD layer. Shikki can steal: hooks-as-extension-points for skills/plugins, a persistent HUD overlay showing active agent state, and the agent-team orchestration pattern for parallel skill execution.

entries.description: Convert documentation websites, GitHub repositories, and PDFs into Claude AI skills with automatic conflict detection.
entries.takeaway: Automated skill ingestion pipeline (URL/PDF/repo → Claude skill) with conflict detection when skills overlap. Shikki can steal: the /ingest pipeline UX for turning external docs into skills, and the conflict-detection mechanism to warn when new skills shadow existing ones.

entries.description: Claude Code notifications without the context switch. A minimal, always-present session manager for macOS.
entries.takeaway: Native Swift macOS app providing a minimal, always-present session manager for Claude Code with notifications that avoid context switching. Shikki can steal: the 'island' UX pattern (persistent ambient status widget), non-intrusive notification design for long-running AI sessions, and the Swift-native macOS integration approach.

entries.description: Native macOS menu bar app to use your Claude Code & ChatGPT subscriptions with AI coding tools.
entries.takeaway: Swift macOS menu bar app that proxies Claude Code / ChatGPT subscriptions for third-party AI tools. Shikki can steal: the subscription-proxy pattern for routing AI provider calls, and the menu bar entry point as a lightweight Shikki session launcher without a full terminal.

entries.description: An open-source, code-first Go toolkit for building, evaluating, and deploying sophisticated AI agents.
entries.takeaway: Google's Agent Development Kit in Go: code-first agent definition, built-in evaluation harness, deployment tooling. Shikki can steal: the evaluation/scoring framework for agents (run agent, measure output quality), the code-first agent definition syntax, and the structured deployment pipeline pattern.

entries.description: Open-source framework for augmenting humans using AI with modular prompt system.
entries.takeaway: Fabric organizes AI capabilities as named 'patterns' — discrete, composable prompt modules invokable from the CLI. Shikki can steal: the patterns-as-skills naming convention, the CLI piping model (stdin → pattern → stdout), and the community pattern library approach for Shikki skill distribution.

entries.description: Compilation of extracted system prompts from ChatGPT, Claude, Gemini, Grok, Perplexity, and others. Updated regularly.
entries.takeaway: Study how frontier AI products structure their system prompts — persona, constraints, tool descriptions, escalation paths. Useful reference for refining Shikki's own agent system prompts and @persona definitions.

entries.description: Use Claude Code for free in the terminal, VSCode extension or via Discord.
entries.takeaway: Alternative Claude Code entrypoints (Discord bot, VSCode extension). Shikki can observe which surfaces users gravitate toward when the primary CLI is inaccessible — informs potential Shikki multi-surface strategy.

entries.description: A command-line tool and Xcode Extension for formatting Swift code.
entries.takeaway: Mature Swift CLI + Xcode Extension dual-mode tool. Shikki can study its CLI flag design, config file conventions (.swiftformat), and how it handles the CLI↔IDE plugin duality for its own Swift CLI architecture.

entries.description: Cross-platform smart notifications plugin for Claude Code with webhook support.
entries.takeaway: Lightweight notification plugin for Claude Code with webhook integration. Shikki can steal: the plugin-as-sidecar pattern (attach to existing tool via hooks), webhook-out for external integrations like Slack/Discord, and the cross-platform notification abstraction.

top3_highlights.why: Highest signal: hooks + agent teams + HUD is exactly the composability model Shikki is building. Study the hook event taxonomy immediately.
Direct overlap with Shikki's /ingest pipeline. The conflict-detection mechanism is a gap in Shikki today — steal it.
Fabric's patterns-as-skills + stdin/stdout piping is the CLI interaction model Shikki should emulate for skill invocation from the terminal.
